COMPUTER VISION — Final Project Brief Saint Joseph University · Spring 2026

Teams: up to 3 students
Duration: 1 month
Dataset: freely chosen (public benchmark or custom)
Language: Python or C++; any framework
Deliverables: GitHub repo, written report, demo video, live defense

Design and implement a Computer Vision system that solves a real or well-defined
problem of your choice. You are free to use classical approaches, deep learning, or a
combination of both. You are not required to cover all CV topics — depth and quality on a
focused problem is more valuable than a shallow treatment of many techniques.

Choose any problem that can be addressed with computer vision. Examples include (but
are not limited to):
Object detection or recognition in a specific domain (medical, industrial, aerial,
etc.)
Scene segmentation for autonomous driving or robotics
Multi-object tracking in video
Image classification with limited data (few-shot, self-supervised)
Anomaly or defect detection
Pose estimation or action recognition
Any other problem you find meaningful and technically challenging
Your chosen problem must be specific enough that results can be measured quantitatively.

Your project must include at least the following:
A clear problem formulation and motivation
A chosen approach — classical CV, deep learning (CNN, transformer, etc.), or hybrid
— with justification
A training or evaluation pipeline on a real dataset
Quantitative evaluation with appropriate metrics for your task (accuracy, mAP,
mIoU, MOTA, etc.)
At least one experiment showing the effect of a design choice (architecture, loss,
augmentation, threshold, etc.)
Visualization of results (predictions, failure cases, attention maps, etc.)

Any public dataset or data you collect yourself
Must be clearly documented: source, size, class distribution, split strategy
(train/val/test)
Minimum 500 labelled samples; more is expected for detection and segmentation
tasks

README with project description, setup instructions, and how to run inference
Clean, modular code with comments
requirements.txt or environment.yml
Link to weights or instructions to reproduce training
Written Report (6–10 pages)

Demo Video (2–4 minutes)
Show your system running on real inputs
Narrate or annotate what is happening at each step
Live Defense (10 min presentation + 5 min Q&A)
All team members present and answer questions
Be ready to justify every design decision

Photosynthesis is the process plants use to convert light energy into chemical energy.
This process allows plants to produce glucose, which stores energy that supports growth and survival.
Photosynthesis mainly takes place in the chloroplasts of plant cells.
Chlorophyll, the green pigment inside chloroplasts, helps capture sunlight from the environment.
Water absorbed by the roots and carbon dioxide from the air are key inputs used during photosynthesis.
Using these inputs and sunlight, plants produce glucose and release oxygen as an output.
Photosynthesis is important because it supports plant life, contributes oxygen to the atmosphere, and helps sustain food chains.

Locating Keypoints Key points are extracted at different scales and blur levels and all subsequent computations are performed within the scale space framework. For each key-point (corner) we need to find its best scale which have maximum value (cornerness measure). It is achieved by comparing same corner with its neighbors of above and lower scales and select scale with maximum value. For same image, it is not necessary for its corners to be localized at same scale. This will make the descriptors invariant to image scaling and small changes in perspective. |

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Segmentation as Thresholding Thresholding is one of the easiest methods of image segmentation where a threshold is set for dividing pixels into two classes. Pixels that have values greater than the threshold value are set to 1 while pixels with values lesser than the threshold value are set to 0. The image is thus converted into a binary map, resulting in the process often termed binarization. Image thresholding is very useful in case the difference in pixel values between the two target classes is very high, and it is easy to choose an average value as the threshold. Thresholding is often used for image binarization so that further algorithms like contour detection and identification that work only on binary images can be used | 
 |

ten used for image binarization so that further algorithms like contour detection and identification that work only on binary images can be used |

Segmentation as Thresholding / Otsu’s method • Once you understand global thresholding, the next natural question is: “Can the computer choose the best threshold value for me?” • That is exactly what Otsu’s method tries to do. Instead of manually picking a number like 120 or 160 • Otsu looks at the entire image histogram and finds a threshold that best separates the pixels into two groups: background and foreground. • It aims to find the threshold value that maximizes the between-class variance, essentially creating the best possible separation between the two classes (foreground and background). |

As cyber threats grow more sophisticated, traditional rule-based security systems have proven insufficient, driving researchers to explore artificial intelligence (AI) as a stronger alternative. This literature review examines three sources: “Threat Detection Driven by Artificial Intelligence” by Chen et al. (2024), “Artificial Intelligence in Cybersecurity” by Wirkuttis and Klein (2017), and “XAI for Cybersecurity” by Srivastava et al. (2022). Although each source approaches the topic differently — covering ML algorithms, security frameworks, and model explainability respectively — they collectively suggest that AI significantly enhances cybersecurity capabilities, but that its effective deployment depends on addressing persistent challenges around data quality, transparency, and human oversight.

All three articles agree that conventional cybersecurity tools are no longer adequate. Chen et al. (2024) explain that attacks have evolved from simple viruses into advanced persistent threats and ransomware, rendering signature-based detection — which depends on recognizing known threats — ineffective against new variants. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) add that traditional intrusion detection systems suffer from low detection rates, slow throughput, and an inability to adapt to changing environments without constant human adjustment. Srivastava et al. (2022) frame the consequences in concrete terms, noting that the average cost of a data breach has reached millions of dollars globally, and that even established frameworks like those from NIST and ISO are increasingly bypassed by attackers. The agreement across all three sources — spanning seven years of research — signals that the field
chunk.section_heading: The Failure of Traditional Security Tools

chunk.chunk_text: IST and ISO are increasingly bypassed by attackers. The agreement across all three sources — spanning seven years of research — signals that the field has moved past questioning whether traditional tools are sufficient, and toward determining how AI should replace them.
chunk.section_heading: The Failure of Traditional Security Tools

chunk.chunk_text: Having identified the gap left by traditional tools, the articles collectively present AI as its most viable replacement. Chen et al. (2024) detail how algorithms such as K-Means, LSTM networks, and CNNs enable real-time detection of unknown threats through behavioral analysis and network traffic monitoring, with AI-based IDS systems continuously adapting to new attack patterns in ways static systems cannot. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) highlight that deep neural networks can now predict cyberattacks before they occur, shifting organizations from a reactive to an anticipatory security model. Srivastava et al. (2022) extend this further, showing how AI tools are being applied across healthcare, banking, and industrial systems — sectors where the stakes of a breach are especially high. Though each source emphasizes a different application, all three point to the same underlying shift:
chunk.section_heading: AI as a More Capable Defense

chunk.chunk_text:
where the stakes of a breach are especially high. Though each source emphasizes a different application, all three point to the same underlying shift: security is moving from passive, rules-based gatekeeping toward active, intelligent threat identification.
Limitations and the Need for Explainability
None of the three sources treat AI as a complete solution. Chen et al. (2024) warn that models are only as reliable as their training data, and that poor labeling or inconsistent formats can cause AI to learn incorrect patterns. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) argue that AI still lacks the contextual judgment needed for full autonomy, making human oversight essential. Srivastava et al. (2022) offer the most detailed critique, pointing to adversarial vulnerabilities and the black-box nature of most AI systems — where no reasoning is provided for decisions — as critical liabilities in security
chunk.section_heading: AI as a More Capable Defense

chunk.chunk_text: arial vulnerabilities and the black-box nature of most AI systems — where no reasoning is provided for decisions — as critical liabilities in security contexts. They propose Explainable AI (XAI), using tools such as SHAP and LIME, as the solution. While the other two articles do not use this term, their shared emphasis on human collaboration reflects the same underlying need. Together, these perspectives make clear that the next frontier in AI-driven cybersecurity is not just higher accuracy, but greater transparency and accountability.
chunk.section_heading: AI as a More Capable Defense

chunk.chunk_text: The three articles together present a clear trajectory: conventional security tools have failed to keep pace with modern threats, AI has emerged as a powerful and necessary alternative, but its deployment introduces new challenges around data quality, autonomy, and interpretability. All three sources ultimately converge on the same conclusion — the most effective cybersecurity approach combines AI’s speed and scale with human judgment and oversight. Future research could explore how explainability frameworks such as SHAP and LIME can be embedded into active security systems without compromising performance.

As digital infrastructure becomes increasingly central to daily life, the security systems designed to protect it face mounting pressure from threats that are more frequent, sophisticated, and difficult to predict. Researchers have turned to artificial intelligence as a promising avenue for strengthening cybersecurity practices. This literature review draws on three key sources: "Threat Detection Driven by Artificial Intelligence" by Chen et al. (2024), "Artificial Intelligence in Cybersecurity" by Wirkuttis and Klein (2017), and "XAI for Cybersecurity" by Srivastava et al. (2022). While these studies differ in focus, they share a common thread: AI holds transformative potential for cybersecurity, yet demands careful consideration of its limitations, particularly around transparency and human oversight.

Body Paragraph 1: Why Traditional Security Tools Are No Longer Sufficient
A central concern shared across the three articles is that conventional cybersecurity tools have become incapable of keeping pace with today's threat environment. Chen et al. (2024) describe how attacks have shifted from simple malware to threats like ransomware and advanced persistent threats, rendering signature-based detection systems — built around the assumption of known threats — largely ineffective. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) reinforce this, noting that static systems cannot self-adjust to unfamiliar attack patterns, and that human response alone introduces dangerous delays. Srivastava et al. (2022) contextualize the problem further, framing the urgency to adopt AI as a direct consequence of how badly conventional tools have struggled. The consistency of this critique across all three sources signals that

As digital infrastructure becomes increasingly central to daily life, the security systems designed to protect it face mounting pressure from threats that are more frequent, sophisticated, and difficult to predict. Researchers have turned to artificial intelligence as a promising avenue for strengthening cybersecurity practices. This literature review draws on three key sources: "Threat Detection Driven by Artificial Intelligence" by Chen et al. Convergence depends on:

Several structural issues limit the efficiency of basic gradient descent.
If the level sets of f are highly elongated, the gradient may point toward steep directions
rather than toward the minimum, leading to slow progress.
The same step size is applied to all coordinates, even if the function varies at very different
scales along different directions.
In nonconvex problems:
convergence to saddle points is possible,
different initializations may lead to different solutions.
These limitations motivate modified update rules and improved step-size strategies.

Momentum
Basic gradient descent updates the parameters using only the current gradient.
Momentum introduces a running velocity:
vk+1 = βvk + ∇f(xk),
xk+1 = xk −ηvk+1,
with β ∈(0, 1).
The update accumulates gradient information over time, leading to smoother trajectories.
This reduces oscillations and accelerates progress along directions of consistent descent.

Gradient descent applies the same step size to all coordinates:

When different coordinates have different scales, this can lead to inefficient zig-zagging

A more general update takes the form

Such transformations rescale directions of descent to better match the geometry of the

combine scaling with momentum.
These approaches reduce sensitivity to parameter tuning and improve robustness in
large-scale problems.

chunk.chunk_text:
Remote Desktop Services (RDS) is a Microsoft Windows Server role that allows users to remotely access desktops, applications, and data from any device over a network.
🖥️
Remote Access
Access a full Windows desktop or specific applications from any location or device.
🌐
Network Based
Works over LAN, WAN, or internet using the RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) on port 3389.
👥
Multi-User
Multiple users can connect simultaneously to the same Windows Server session.

Any Device
Windows / Mac / Mobile

Port 3389
🌐

Encrypted
Tunnel
🖥️

How it works: The client sends keyboard/mouse input to the server over the network. The server processes everything and sends back only the screen image. All applications and data remain on the server — never on the client device.

The core component. Hosts the desktop sessions and applications that remote users connect to.
🔑
RD Licensing
Manages Client Access Licenses (CALs) required for users to connect to RDS legally.
🌐
RD Gateway
Allows secure RDP connections over the internet through HTTPS without a VPN.
🔗

Provides a web portal where users access RemoteApp programs and desktops via a browser.
⚖️

Load-balances user connections across multiple RD Session Host servers for high availability.
💾
Hosts virtual machine-based desktops using Hyper-V for VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure).

💰
Cost Savings
Thin clients are cheaper than full PCs. Software licenses are centralized.
🔒
Enhanced Security
Data never leaves the server. Full control over user access and permissions.
🏠
Remote Work
Employees work from anywhere — home, travel, or any office worldwide.
⚙️
Easy Management
IT manages one server instead of hundreds of individual workstations.
📈
Scalability
Easily add users or resources without upgrading every endpoint device.
🔄
Disaster Recovery
Data is centralized, making backups and recovery fast and reliable.
chunk.section_heading: Why organizations choose Remote Desktop Services

chunk.chunk_text:
Requires users to authenticate before a full RDP session is established, protecting against unauthorized access.
🔒
SSL/TLS Encryption:
All data between client and server is encrypted. In our lab, a self-signed certificate was used (normal for testing).
🧱

Port 3389 must be explicitly opened. We ran netsh commands to allow RDP traffic through the firewall.
👤
User Permissions:
Only users in the 'Remote Desktop Users' group can connect. We created a dedicated rdpuser account.
🔑

Production environments require Client Access Licenses. Our lab uses the 120-day grace period.
🌐
For connections over the internet, RD Gateway tunnels RDP through HTTPS port 443 for added security.

Lab Environment
Hypervisor:
Oracle VirtualBox
Server OS:

Server Name:
Touma
Server IP:
192.168.1.16
Client OS:
Windows 11 (Host PC)
Protocol:
RDP — Port 3389
RDS Role:

Test User:
rdpuser (custom account)
Connection Flow
Create rdpuser account

Run mstsc on client PC
Enter Server IP & credentials
✅ Connected via RDP!
mstsc (Remote Desktop Connection) opened on host PC → Server IP 192.168.1.16 entered → VM ipconfig shown in background confirming matching IP
chunk.section_heading: Lab Demo — Step 1: Initiating the RDP Connection

chunk.chunk_text: Windows Security prompt → rdpuser account entered → connecting to 192.168.1.16 (the Windows Server 2016 VM)

⚠️ This is normal in a lab environment. The server uses a self-signed SSL certificate (not from a trusted CA). In production, organizations use trusted certificates from providers like DigiCert or Let's Encrypt. We clicked 'Yes' to proceed.

Report — Distributed Big Data Environment, Streaming Analytics, and AI Search
MongoDB Replica Set • Redpanda/Kafka • Time-Series Events • Qdrant • Streamlit

The Smart Marketplace POC simulates the data backbone of an online marketplace. A product catalog is stored in MongoDB, user interactions are generated as streaming events, those events are distributed through a Kafka-compatible topic, consumers persist them into MongoDB, analytics are computed on top of the stored data, and AI is used to support semantic product search.
The project was built as a fully local environment so that installation, usage, validation, and failover can all be demonstrated in a controlled way on one machine. This makes the POC strong from a theoretical point of view because the core properties of a big data environment are visible end to end.

Figure 1 - local architecture of the Smart Marketplace.
The distributed system is composed of five logical layers: a product data layer, a streaming layer, a consumer processing layer, a distributed storage layer, and an AI search layer.
Table 1 - Distributed system components
Table 2 - 3v's of big data
chunk.section_heading: 3. HOW THE ARCHITECTURE ANSWERS THE 3 V’S OF BIG DATA

chunk.chunk_text:
4.1 Replica set and replication
MongoDB was deployed as a three-node replica set. In a replica set, one node is the primary and accepts writes, while the remaining nodes replicate the data as secondaries. If the primary fails, the set elects a new primary. This is the theoretical basis for high availability and fault tolerance in the project.
4.2 Streaming and partitioned event flow
Marketplace events are not written directly from the producer into the database. They are first published to the events topic. Because the topic has three partitions and the consumer group has three members, the workload can be processed in parallel while preserving ordering within each partition.
4.3 Time-series storage and aggregation
The raw event stream is stored in a MongoDB time-series collection called events_ts. Time-series collections are optimized for timestamped measurements and are a natural fit
chunk.section_heading: 4. THEORY BEHIND THE STRUCTURE AND DEPLOYMENT

chunk.chunk_text:
stored in a MongoDB time-series collection called events_ts. Time-series collections are optimized for timestamped measurements and are a natural fit for event logs such as product views and clicks. A second collection, product_stats, stores derived counters so that analytics queries can run faster.
4.4 Simple orchestration
Docker Compose was used to keep the environment simple. All services are declared in one compose file, which makes the stack easy to start, easy to stop, and easy to reproduce.
chunk.section_heading: 4. THEORY BEHIND THE STRUCTURE AND DEPLOYMENT

chunk.chunk_text:
All commands below are executed from the project root.
Step 1 — Start the distributed stack
This starts the MongoDB replica set nodes, Redpanda, Kafka UI, the replica-set initializer, Qdrant, and the three event consumers.
Step 2 — Load the marketplace product catalog
This loads the product catalog into mini_bigdata_real.products.
Step 3 — Produce marketplace events
This generates marketplace interactions and publishes them to the events topic.
Step 4 — Run analytics
This validates that the time-series collection and the derived statistics can be queried in a meaningful way.
Step 5 — Run the Streamlit dashboard
This opens a small visual interface for counts, semantic search, and controlled operational actions.
chunk.section_heading: 5. PRACTICAL EXECUTION IN A FEW STEPS

chunk.chunk_text:
A simple demonstration scenario was used: load the product catalog, publish a large number of events, let the consumers write them into MongoDB, verify analytics, then test failover by stopping the primary node.
Table 3 - observed results
The failover test showed that the primary node could be stopped, a new primary could be elected automatically, writes could continue, and the original node could rejoin later as a secondary. This directly supports the theoretical explanation of replication and availability.

AI became part of the platform through semantic search.
7.1 Semantic search design
The AI layer embeds each product into a vector representation using the sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model. The embedding is built mainly from the searchText field, complemented by title, category, brand, and specs. Those vectors are stored in Qdrant, while MongoDB remains the source of truth for the full product documents.
7.2 Query flow
When a user enters a search query, the query is embedded with the same model, Qdrant retrieves the nearest vectors, and the matching productIds are used to fetch full product details from MongoDB. This makes search more intelligent than exact keyword matching and turns the marketplace into a smart retrieval system.
7.3 Why this AI layer fits the POC
The AI extension remains simple enough to run locally, but it is still meaningful: it demonstrates semantic

system.
7.3 Why this AI layer fits the POC
The AI extension remains simple enough to run locally, but it is still meaningful: it demonstrates semantic retrieval, a practical smart-marketplace feature, without replacing the distributed data architecture already built.

We designed this project to build a Road Accident Risk Analysis System using MongoDB as the database platform and machine learning as the AI component. Our system analyzes accident records, supports scalable storage and querying in a Big Data context, and integrates AI for predictive analytics.
We focused on two main objectives:

The project combines:
a NoSQL database layer to store and process large volumes of accident records
an analytics layer based on aggregation and geospatial concepts
an AI layer for accident severity prediction
This approach makes our project relevant to both Enterprise Data Management and Big Data analytics.
chunk.section_heading: 1. Predict accident severity 2. Identify high-risk locations

chunk.chunk_text:
The main objectives of our project are to:
store road accident data in MongoDB
explore the suitability of MongoDB for Big Data applications
explain MongoDB architecture, deployment, replication, consistency, and availability
analyze accident patterns using database queries and aggregation concepts
predict accident severity using machine learning
identify high-risk areas using location-related information
demonstrate how MongoDB can support volume, velocity, and variety

The dataset we used in this project is a road accident dataset stored in a CSV file called Road Accident Data.csv
It contains approximately 307,973 rows and 23 columns. Each row represents one accident record, while the columns describe different characteristics of the accident.
The most important attributes in the dataset include:
Accident severity
Accident date
Time
Latitude
Longitude
Speed limit
Weather conditions
Road surface conditions
Road type
Light conditions
Junction control
Junction detail
Urban or rural area
Vehicle type
Number of casualties
Number of vehicles
Local authority district
This dataset is suitable for both database storage and machine learning because it contains:
structured numerical data
categorical data
temporal data
geospatial data
We divided our system into three main layers:
We used MongoDB as the database system for storing and managing accident records.
We used MongoDB queries, indexing, aggregation, and geospatial concepts to analyze accident patterns and identify hotspots.
We used machine learning models to predict accident severity based on accident conditions.

We selected MongoDB because it is a document-oriented NoSQL database that is well suited for large and flexible datasets.
Its main advantages include:
schema flexibility
strong support for semi-structured data
indexing support
an aggregation framework
geospatial capabilities
replication and sharding support
cloud deployment through MongoDB Atlas
This makes MongoDB appropriate for our system, since it handles accident records with mixed data types and can support future Big Data growth.

MongoDB is relevant in Big Data because it can address the three main Big Data dimensions.
6.1 Volume
We can scale MongoDB horizontally through sharding, which allows data to be distributed across multiple machines.
6.2 Velocity
MongoDB supports modern stream-oriented architectures and can be integrated with near-real-time ingestion scenarios. In our project, the dataset is historical, but we can extend the architecture to support streaming accident feeds.
6.3 Variety
Our accidents dataset contains a mixture of:
numerical features
text categories
date and time data
geospatial coordinates
MongoDB’s flexible document model is well suited to handle this variety.

MongoDB provides several mechanisms to support scalability, availability, and reliability in Big Data environments. In our project, these concepts are demonstrated through replication, sharding, and configurable read/write operations.
chunk.section_heading: 7. MONGODB THEORY COVERED IN THE PROJECT:

chunk.chunk_text: We store data in MongoDB as documents grouped into collections, unlike relational databases, which store data in rows and tables.

We can deploy MongoDB as:
a standalone server
a replica set
a sharded cluster
7.3 Replication
Replication in MongoDB is implemented using replica sets, where data is duplicated across multiple nodes. A replica set consists of a primary node, which handles write operations, and one or more secondary nodes, which replicate the data from the primary.
This architecture ensures high availability and fault tolerance. In the event of a primary node failure, MongoDB automatically performs a failover by electing one of the secondary nodes as the new primary. This guarantees that the system remains operational without data loss.

In our MongoDB project, replication applies to the entire road_accidents database rather than to individual records or collections. The accidents collection containing the road accident dataset and the hotspots collection containing the derived risk locations are replicated across replica set members. In MongoDB Atlas, this replication is managed automatically by the platform. Data is written to the primary node and then synchronized to secondary nodes. If the primary node becomes unavailable, MongoDB can elect a new primary, ensuring service continuity and improving availability.
7.4 Sharding
Sharding is a technique used by MongoDB to achieve horizontal scaling by distributing data across multiple servers. Instead of storing all data on a single machine, MongoDB divides the dataset into smaller parts called shards, each stored on a different server.
This approach allows the system to
chunk.section_heading: Replication of the Road Accident Dataset

chunk.chunk_text:
a single machine, MongoDB divides the dataset into smaller parts called shards, each stored on a different server.
This approach allows the system to handle very large datasets efficiently and improves performance by distributing the workload. A shard key is used to determine how data is partitioned across shards.
In the context of this project, sharding can be conceptually applied by distributing accident data based on fields such as geographic location or time. For example, queries filtering by district demonstrate how data can be efficiently accessed:
results = col.find({"Local_Authority_District": "Kensington and Chelsea"})
This illustrates how data distribution can improve query performance in large-scale systems.
In our project, sharding was demonstrated conceptually due to limitations of the MongoDB Atlas free-tier cluster. In a real deployment, data could be distributed across
chunk.section_heading: Replication of the Road Accident Dataset

chunk.chunk_text:
arding was demonstrated conceptually due to limitations of the MongoDB Atlas free-tier cluster. In a real deployment, data could be distributed across multiple shards using a shard key such as geographic location. Queries filtering on this key would be efficiently routed to the relevant shard, improving performance and scalability.
7.5 Consistency and Availability Configuration
MongoDB allows flexible configuration to balance consistency, availability, and performance. This is achieved through read concern, write concern, and read preference.
chunk.section_heading: Replication of the Road Accident Dataset

chunk.chunk_text:
In this project, we demonstrated how MongoDB can be configured to favor data consistency over performance. This is particularly important in applications where accurate and reliable data is critical.
To achieve strong consistency, MongoDB provides configuration options such as read preference, read concern, and write concern.
First, the connection was configured to always read from the primary node using the PRIMARY read preference. This ensures that all read operations return the most up-todate data.
Second, both read concern and write concern were set to "majority". This means that:
Write operations are only considered successful when confirmed by a majority of replica set members.
Read operations return only data that has been fully replicated across the majority of nodes.
The following code demonstrates this configuration:
The successful insertion and retrieval of the document

The following code demonstrates this configuration:
The successful insertion and retrieval of the document confirm that the operation was executed under strong consistency guarantees.
This configuration ensures that the system prioritizes data accuracy and reliability, even if it introduces slightly higher latency. Such an approach is essential in critical systems where incorrect or outdated data cannot be tolerated.
7.5.2 Favoring Availability and Performance
In addition to consistency, MongoDB allows the system to be configured to favor availability and performance. This is useful in applications where fast response times and continuous system access are more important than strict data consistency.
To achieve higher availability, MongoDB provides flexible configuration options such as read preference, read concern, and write concern.
First,

To achieve higher availability, MongoDB provides flexible configuration options such as read preference, read concern, and write concern.
First, the system can be configured to allow reads from secondary nodes using the SECONDARY read preference. This distributes the read workload across multiple nodes and improves system availability.
Second, the write concern can be reduced to w=1, meaning that a write operation is acknowledged as soon as the primary node confirms it, without waiting for replication to other nodes.
Third, the read concern can be set to "local", allowing faster reads even if the data has not yet been fully replicated.

MongoDB supports geospatial storage and querying, which makes it relevant for location-based accident analysis.

We used MongoDB Atlas as a cloud-based database platform to store and manage the road accident dataset. We performed the following steps:
created a MongoDB Atlas account
created a free cluster for cloud deployment
created a database user with authentication credentials
added an IP address to allow a secure connection
MongoDB Atlas provides us with a managed cloud environment that ensures scalability, availability, and security without requiring manual server configuration.
10.1 Connecting using MongoDB Compass
We used MongoDB Compass as a graphical interface to interact with the database. We completed the following steps:
downloaded and installed MongoDB Compass
obtained the connection string from MongoDB Atlas
pasted the connection string into MongoDB Compass  successfully established the connection to the cluster
This allowed us to manage the database more easily, including data

tring into MongoDB Compass  successfully established the connection to the cluster
This allowed us to manage the database more easily, including data import, querying, and analysis.
10.2 Connecting using Python (PyMongo)
We used Python to connect to MongoDB programmatically through the PyMongo library. We completed the following steps:
installed PyMongo library
established a connection to MongoDB Atlas using the connection string
accessed the database and collections through Python
This connection allowed us to perform more advanced data processing, including aggregation pipelines and hotspot detection analysis.

Here we demonstrate replication in MongoDB Atlas.
We run the hello command, which gives us information about the cluster.
As we can see, the cluster has a replica set, shown by the setName field.
We also see multiple hosts, which means the data is replicated across multiple nodes.
The field isWritablePrimary indicates the primary node, which handles write operations.
This confirms that our dataset is replicated across multiple servers, ensuring high availability and fault tolerance.

We used MongoDB as a document-oriented database, where data is stored in collections as JSON-like documents. In our project, we stored the data in the "accidents" collection inside the "road_accidents" database.
Each document represents a single road accident and contains several attributes that describe the event. These attributes include information such as accident severity, weather conditions, road surface conditions, speed limit, time of the accident, and geographic location.

We used MongoDB’s aggregation framework to analyze the road accident dataset and extract meaningful insights. We implemented the following queries:
 Accidents by Severity
This query groups accidents according to severity levels such as Slight, Serious, and Fatal, then counts how many accidents fall into each category. It helps us understand the overall level of accident danger.
 Accidents by Weather Conditions
This query examines how weather conditions affect accident frequency. It highlights conditions such as rain, fog, and dry weather.
 Accidents by Road Type  Accidents by Speed Limit
Each query uses grouping and sorting operations to summarize large amounts of data efficiently. We then exported the results and visualized them using charts to better understand accident patterns.
These analyses demonstrate MongoDB’s ability to process large datasets through its aggregation

em using charts to better understand accident patterns.
These analyses demonstrate MongoDB’s ability to process large datasets through its aggregation framework.

To identify high-risk locations, we implemented a hotspot detection analysis using MongoDB aggregation and Python with PyMongo.
Our approach included the following steps:
rounding geographic coordinates (latitude and longitude) to group nearby accident locations
aggregating accidents based on these grouped locations  calculating a risk score based on accident severity: o Slight accidents → weight 1 o Serious accidents → weight 3 o Fatal accidents → weight 10
This scoring system gives greater importance to more severe accidents.
We then sorted the results in descending order based on the risk score, and we identified the top 50 most dangerous locations. These locations were stored in a separate collection called "hotspots".
Our hotspot analysis revealed areas with a high concentration of accidents and high severity levels. For example:
some locations recorded more than 250

ur hotspot analysis revealed areas with a high concentration of accidents and high severity levels. For example:
some locations recorded more than 250 accidents
several hotspots included multiple fatal accidents
high-risk areas were concentrated in urban zones with dense traffic
The highest-risk location reached a risk score of more than 400, which indicates a significant level of danger.
These results show that MongoDB can be used not only for data storage, but also for advanced analytical tasks such as risk assessment and pattern detection.

14.1 Objective
The goal of this component is to predict accident severity (Slight, Serious, Fatal) using accident-related features. We treated this as a supervised classification problem.

We used variables such as:
Speed limit, weather, road conditions
Light conditions, junction details
Urban/rural area, vehicle type
Day, month, hour
Number of casualties and vehicles
These features describe the accident context.
14.3 Preprocessing
Missing values:
o Numerical → median o Categorical → most frequent
Categorical variables → One-Hot Encoding

Time converted to Hour

The dataset was split into:
80% training

Random Forest → captures complex relationships

To address this:  class_weight = "balanced" was used in both models.
14.8 Results

Random Forest accuracy: 0.8004
Logistic Regression accuracy: 0.5230
The dataset is imbalanced, making minority classes harder to predict.

Models were saved using joblib, including:

14.10 Conclusion:
We can use machine learning to predict accident severity based on accident conditions.
In general, Random Forest performs better, but the results are influenced by class imbalance.

Our project demonstrates how MongoDB and AI can be combined in a Road Accident Risk Analysis System. We chose MongoDB because it provides flexibility, scalability, and support for analytical and geospatial use cases in a Big Data context. Our system design shows how accident data can be stored, queried, aggregated, and extended with predictive analytics. MongoDB also supports important enterprise features such as replication for high availability, sharding for horizontal scalability, and configurable read/write operations to balance consistency and performance. Using MongoDB Atlas further simplified deployment while still providing these advanced capabilities.
The AI component successfully showed that accident severity prediction can be approached as a supervised classification problem using accident conditions as input features. We trained and compared two machine learning models and

approached as a supervised classification problem using accident conditions as input features. We trained and compared two machine learning models and used visual evaluation to analyze their performance.
Overall, our project provides a strong proof of concept for an intelligent road accident analytics platform that could later be extended with live data ingestion, hotspot mapping, and database-integrated predictions.

Smart Teacher Assistant is an AI-powered backend for education workflows.

The application provides agent endpoints for summarizing documents, creating slide decks, and generating quizzes.

The chat endpoint answers questions using retrieved document context. It should only answer from indexed documents and say "Not found in document" when the answer is missing from the context.

The retrieval flow indexes local files from the knowledge base folder, splits them into chunks, stores embeddings in a local JSON vector store, and returns the most relevant chunks for a user question.
